speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the-- this picture is my wonderful couple who, thanks to them, I survived the war. Her name is Michalina Janicki, and her husband's name is Cyprian Janicki. And thanks a lot to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken-- this, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, I assume, yeah-- maybe in [INAUDIBLE] maybe in [INAUDIBLE]. I really don't know him from where this picture taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know what year it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unless it's on the back of the picture there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken right before you lived with them do you think, or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: She send me the picture, so I guess she send me when I left her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe she gave me the picture before I [? ever ?] [? might have ?] [? gone. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was a policeman, [INAUDIBLE] later on-- Wonderful people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Never forget them, till my [? end. ?] And this picture-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, I should say, really, that this is the picture of my beautiful family, which I survived with. The first-- her name is Michalina Janicki, and then her-- the husband's name is Janicki Cyprian. And then I am in between the two of them, and my name was Henya Shulakowska at that time. Then comes two of their sons. One-- the oldest son's name is Henek Janicki, and the younger one is Roma Janicki. And I thank them an awful lot for-- for saving my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Polish family that saved your life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we want to thank them, and we want to thank you very much for a wonderful, wonderful interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you very much for doing that for-- to me. That's it? To my-- I'm Mrs. Henson. To my right is-- to my left, rather, is my husband, Leon Henson. Then comes my son, Elliot and and Susan, his wife. My-- and his children, Michael, Matthew. Then is my youngest son, Bernard, and his wife and his three children, Jeremy, Max, and Amy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Melinda, his wife, Melinda, yeah, OK. And so is Susan, Matthew's wife. I'd like to say thank you to Susie, [INAUDIBLE], Natasha,

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasha, [INAUDIBLE], for coming and doing-- doing such a beautiful job on the tape [? I hope. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --spending the whole day here with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan's mentioned, yeah. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Max Henson, grandson from my--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Leon and Enid Henson. And I would like to say I love them both very much, and-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Matthew. I'm Elliot and Susan's son and Enid and Leon's grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Susan Henson, Enid and Leon's daughter-in-law. And I'd like to thank, from the bottom of my heart-- and I think I speak for my whole family as well-- the-- how grateful we are, and how wonderful it is what they did here today. It wasn't an easy task. We know that. We love them for doing it. And we'd also like to thank all the survivors who participated in this program. It's very important for us as a civilization and all our future generations to know what happened here, not only to this family, but to all people persecuted during the war and at all times. Again, thank you, and we love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm Michael. I'm son of Elliot and Susan and grandson of Leon and Enid. And I'd like to just follow up on what my mother said. We all know what revisionist history has done and can do, and this project and my grandparents' part in it has-- has helped prevent that corrosive effect of saying that this horrible thing never happened. And for our personal families to come, my-- my children, my grandchildren, my brother's children and grandchildren, my cousins' children and grandchildren, and so on, to-- to look at my grandparents and see them retelling their stories-- which has just been so important to me and constituting who I am as a person. I thank them. And it took a bit of cajoling, but it worked. They did it in the end, and I love them very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Elliot Henson, the oldest son of Enid and Leon. I can't add very much to what my family has already said, so I'm going to pass this to my brother. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Bernard Henson, the youngest son. And just that we're all very honored to be part of the family, and to whoever is viewing this in the future, just that you should know that we are real people, even though we might not have met you in person. And the story you heard from my parents is real, and it's something never to be forgotten. And we're very proud of them, and it's something to remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Melinda Henson. I'm Leon and Enid's daughter-in-law. Again, there's not much more to add to what everybody said other than the fact that, again, we're so proud and-- and pleased that our parents have consented to do this program. We know how important it is. Our little children might not realize how important it is, but as they grow older and they have children of their own, who have children of their own, they'll be able to watch a very special part of our family history. And again, we love them both, and we're very happy that they did this for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say thank you to my children. We love them all so very much, all of them. And thank, I think, again everybody for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to thank Sasha and-- and-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] for the correct pronunciation of her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm going to say it before you. [? All right? ?] Well, I thank my wife, Enid, for the courageous decision to go through with it, because I was the one-- I said it's nothing. But I found out of that day, from 9 o'clock to 8 o'clock in the evening that it's something. Well, I went through so much in my lifetime, [INAUDIBLE] give a little more. And that was the day, the happy day, to reunite with our family in United States. And thankful to the United States again-- I'm repeating myself-- for giving us a home where to raise our children and [? very fine ?] [? that ?] we went to. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, and Amy just wants to say.
